GH2-soar-playbook-field-injection.mechanism: SOAR/auto-remediation playbooks build their containment actions by string-interpolating alert fields (hostname, username, file path, URL, process command line, IOC) directly into API calls, enrichment queries, and shell/PowerShell actions without re-validating that those fields are attacker-controllable. Because many of these fields originate from attacker-influenced telemetry (a User-Agent, a DNS label, a filename on disk, a crafted log line a la Log4Shell's JNDI substitution), a payload planted in the field is expanded into the remediation step itself. The defect is a classic injection sink (CWE-78/CWE-77/CWE-88) reached through the response automation rather than the originally-attacked app, so the playbook executes attacker-directed commands/queries against the assets it was meant to protect, with no analyst in the loop.
GH2-soar-playbook-field-injection.exploitation_primitive: Plant a payload (shell metacharacters, an LDAP/JNDI lookup, a parameter-injection string) in a value you know lands in an alert field, e.g. set a process name or hostname to a command-injection string, then trigger the detection so the playbook interpolates and executes it during isolation/disable/firewall actions.
GH2-soar-playbook-field-injection.mitre_gap: Closest is T1059 (Command and Scripting Interpreter), but that models adversary code execution on a host, not the specific seam where a defender's response orchestration becomes the injection sink for attacker-supplied telemetry. ATT&CK has no technique for response-automation-as-execution-substrate (alert-field-to-action injection); T1059 misses both the trust violation (sanitization assumed at ingestion) and the direction (the security tool acting on the analyst's behalf).

anchors.ref: Barracuda - Threat Spotlight: Log injection attacks
anchors.note: Log injection via User-Agent/Referer/X-Forwarded-For/Authorization headers can lead to RCE when logged values are later interpolated/expanded downstream.

anchors.ref: CVE-2021-44228 (Log4Shell)
anchors.note: Canonical example of an attacker-controlled value in a logged field (a header) being expanded by downstream processing into code execution; same class as alert-field expansion in playbooks.

GH2-soar-playbook-field-injection.detection: Alert on SOAR/orchestration action parameters containing shell metacharacters, template/expression syntax, or URL/JNDI patterns; diff the rendered action command against an allowlisted parameter schema; require parameterized API calls (no string concatenation) and tag any playbook step that consumes a raw alert field for human approval.

references.title: Barracuda - Threat Spotlight: Log injection attacks
MITRE ATT&CK T1059 - Command and Scripting Interpreter

GH2-detection-as-code-rule-poison.mechanism: Detection-as-code pipelines ingest Sigma/YARA/Sentinel rules from shared community/vendor repos and trust that a rule which lints, passes review, and shows green coverage actually detects the TTP named in its title/metadata. The exploitable seam is the rule's filter/exception logic: an attacker-supplied PR adds an over-scoped not-condition, a broken field mapping, or a logic-narrowing exclusion (commonly a left-side wildcard on a command-line, image-path, or User-Agent value the attacker controls) so the rule remains syntactically valid and visible in coverage dashboards while silently failing to match the adversary's exact behavior. Because Sigma whitelists are merged across thousands of downstream SOCs, one poisoned exclusion blinds every consumer that syncs the repo, and the rule's presence suppresses scrutiny.
GH2-detection-as-code-rule-poison.exploitation_primitive: Submit a benign-looking PR to the shared rule corpus that appends an exclusion the adversary's own activity satisfies (e.g. a CommandLine|contains filter or a wildcarded path whitelist), so the merged rule looks complete but excludes your tradecraft.
GH2-detection-as-code-rule-poison.mitre_gap: Closest are T1195.001 (Compromise Software Dependencies and Development Tools) and T1562.001 (Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools), but both assume defeating a control on the victim host. Neither models supply-chain poisoning of the detection content itself, where the rule is delivered, green, and trusted yet logically void; ATT&CK has no technique for coverage-preserving detection sabotage upstream of any single environment.

anchors.ref: Karneades/SigmaFilterCheck
anchors.note: Tool built specifically to find easy-to-bypass whitelists (not-condition values) in Sigma rules, including left-side wildcards on attacker-controlled fields that let adversaries evade detections and mislead defenders' coverage confidence.

anchors.ref: SigmaHQ/sigma main rule repository
anchors.note: Shared community corpus consumed by many SOCs via CI sync; the exclusion/whitelist surface is the poisoning target.

GH2-detection-as-code-rule-poison.detection: Run SigmaFilterCheck-style linting in CI to flag wildcards/over-broad exclusions on attacker-controllable fields; require atomic-test (purple-team) validation that each rule fires on its claimed behavior before merge; alert on PRs that only add filter/not-condition clauses to existing rules; track per-rule true-positive firing over time and flag rules that go silent after edits.

references.title: Karneades/SigmaFilterCheck
MITRE ATT&CK T1562.001 - Impair Defenses: Disable or Modify Tools

GH2-edr-cloud-baseline-driftpoison.mechanism: Cloud-delivered EDR/UEBA continuously retrains a rolling behavioral baseline on streamed endpoint/identity telemetry and treats activity that fits the learned baseline as benign by construction. An attacker with low-and-slow presence drip-feeds malicious-shaped behavior into the training stream at a rate below the model's anomaly/concept-drift threshold (a frog-boiling or causative/poisoning attack), so the rolling baseline incrementally absorbs the behavior until the eventual full-tempo action sits inside a now-normalized envelope and never scores anomalous. This exploits the online-learning data pipeline as an unauthenticated training input: there is no integrity boundary distinguishing honest normal from adversary-shaped normal.
GH2-edr-cloud-baseline-driftpoison.exploitation_primitive: Over days/weeks, gradually introduce small increments of the target behavior (off-hours logons, lateral connections, data-volume bumps) just under the detector's threshold so the model relabels it as normal, then execute at full tempo inside the shifted baseline.
GH2-edr-cloud-baseline-driftpoison.mitre_gap: Closest in ATT&CK Enterprise is T1562.001 (Impair Defenses), but that frames evasion as disabling/modifying the tool, not as poisoning the model's training distribution while it runs normally. The behavior is better captured by MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 (Poison Training Data), which Enterprise ATT&CK does not incorporate; Enterprise has no first-class technique for online-model baseline poisoning of a security detector.

anchors.ref: Apruzzese et al., arXiv:2001.11821 - automated evaluation of robustness to deception, poisoning and concept drift
anchors.note: Studies adversarial vs behavioural defensive AI including poisoning and concept-drift evasion of behaviour-based detectors.

anchors.ref: MITRE ATLAS AML.T0020 - Poison Training Data
anchors.note: ATLAS technique covering corruption of training data to manipulate model behavior; the closest catalogued analogue, absent from Enterprise ATT&CK.

GH2-edr-cloud-baseline-driftpoison.detection: Monitor baseline/model drift velocity and alert on sustained directional shifts in a per-entity baseline; retain immutable raw telemetry for retrospective scoring against a frozen reference model; cross-check online-model verdicts against static signature/threshold rules that are not self-training; flag entities whose 'normal' envelope expanded monotonically over a long window.

references.title: arXiv:2001.11821 - Adversarial vs behavioural-based defensive AI (poisoning, concept drift)
MITRE ATLAS - Poison Training Data (AML.T0020)

GH2-passkey-sync-fabric-recovery-downgra.mechanism: A relying party treats a synced FIDO2/passkey assertion as proof of a phishing-resistant, hardware-bound authenticator, but for synced passkeys the private key is replicated by the platform vendor's cloud (iCloud Keychain, Google Password Manager, 1Password) across every device on the user's account and is recoverable through that account's recovery path. The security boundary therefore collapses from the device's secure element to the platform account: the WebAuthn assertion still verifies, but device possession is no longer implied. Separately, most RPs retain weaker fallback factors (OTP, SMS, security questions) for the same account, so an attacker can drive the login into a non-WebAuthn branch (MFA downgrade) without ever touching the passkey's cryptography.
GH2-passkey-sync-fabric-recovery-downgra.exploitation_primitive: Take over the platform sync account (or its recovery path) to re-provision the synced passkey onto an attacker device, or, where that is hard, manipulate the login flow to trigger the RP's weaker fallback factor and bypass the passkey entirely.
GH2-passkey-sync-fabric-recovery-downgra.mitre_gap: Closest are T1556 (Modify Authentication Process) and T1528 (Steal Application Access Token), but neither captures that a phishing-resistant authenticator's key is cloud-replicated and recoverable, nor the MFA-downgrade-to-fallback path. ATT&CK has no technique distinguishing synced from device-bound credentials or modeling the sync-fabric account as the credential's real trust root.

anchors.ref: Beyond Identity - How Attackers Bypass FIDO: why synced passkeys fail
anchors.note: Documents the synced-passkey account-takeover and MFA-downgrade-to-fallback attack surface.

anchors.ref: WorkOS - Passkeys stop phishing. Your MFA fallbacks undo it.
anchors.note: Explains how retained weaker fallback factors reintroduce the phishing surface passkeys were meant to remove.

GH2-passkey-sync-fabric-recovery-downgra.detection: Inspect WebAuthn authenticator metadata/AAGUID and backup-eligibility/backup-state flags to distinguish synced from device-bound credentials and require step-up for synced ones in high-assurance contexts; alert on new passkey registration immediately after a platform-account recovery; detect and block fallback-factor use on accounts that have an enrolled passkey.

references.title: Beyond Identity - How Attackers Bypass FIDO (synced passkeys)
FIDO Alliance - Passkeys

GH2-device-posture-attestation-spoof.mechanism: Conditional-access/zero-trust IdPs relax controls based on a device-compliance/posture signal (managed, patched, EDR-healthy, disk-encrypted) that is largely computed and asserted by a client-side agent on a device the user fully controls. Because much of the posture (device model, OS build, compliance verdict) is read from registry/local state and reported by the agent rather than hardware-attested, an admin on the endpoint can tamper with the reported values or register a fake/over-permissive device so a non-compliant or attacker-controlled machine presents as healthy. Entra/Intune-style flows also have a check-interval gap: a device flipped non-compliant is not blocked retroactively, so a session established while spoofing remains valid until the next evaluation.
GH2-device-posture-attestation-spoof.exploitation_primitive: Modify the registry/agent state that feeds compliance (e.g. device model), or register an arbitrary device via tooling like pytune/TokenSmith, to satisfy the compliant-device CA branch and obtain a token that waives step-up controls.
GH2-device-posture-attestation-spoof.mitre_gap: Closest are T1556 (Modify Authentication Process) and T1622 (Debugger Evasion is unrelated; T1606 Forge Web Credentials is nearer). None model client-asserted device-posture forgery against conditional access; ATT&CK lacks a technique for spoofing the compliance/attestation signal that an IdP uses to relax MFA, and treats device trust as out of scope.

anchors.ref: OuttaTune - Bypassing Conditional Access in Microsoft Intune (CirriusTech)
anchors.note: Device-model registry spoofing bypasses a compliance CA policy and remains undetected until the next compliance check, with no retroactive block.

anchors.ref: AADInternals - Bypassing conditional access by faking device compliance
anchors.note: Demonstrates marking devices compliant / registering arbitrary device configs to satisfy CA policies.

GH2-device-posture-attestation-spoof.detection: Correlate asserted device model/OS against hardware-rooted attestation (TPM device-health attestation) rather than agent self-report; alert on device registrations from unexpected platform/User-Agent combinations; shorten compliance re-evaluation and enforce continuous access evaluation so a flipped device loses access immediately; flag tokens issued to devices whose posture later fails.

references.title: OuttaTune: Bypassing Conditional Access in Microsoft Intune
AADInternals - Faking device compliance to bypass conditional access

GH2-slsa-provenance-scope-gap.mechanism: An artifact verifier (admission controller, deploy gate, package consumer) treats a valid, correctly-signed SLSA/in-toto provenance attestation as proof the whole artifact was built as described. But an in-toto predicate only binds specific subjects (digests) and a specific modeled build step; it does not necessarily cover pre-build inputs, vendored/transitively-resolved dependencies, post-build mutation, or steps outside the attested step's scope. The seam is the gap between what the predicate cryptographically asserts and what actually mattered to the deployed artifact, so a genuinely verifiable attestation can vouch for an artifact whose malicious portion was never in the attestation's claim.
GH2-slsa-provenance-scope-gap.exploitation_primitive: Inject the malicious logic into a build phase the predicate does not bind (a vendored dependency, a pre-build code-generation step, or a post-build repackage) so the artifact ships with valid provenance that simply never described the compromised component.
GH2-slsa-provenance-scope-gap.mitre_gap: Closest is T1195.002 (Compromise Software Supply Chain), which is broad and direction-agnostic. It does not capture the verification-scope gap, that a cryptographically valid provenance attestation can pass while the exploited step lies outside the predicate's subject/step scope. ATT&CK has no technique for attested-step-not-equal-exploited-step provenance evasion, and treats supply-chain compromise as a single undifferentiated technique.

anchors.ref: SLSA v1.0 Distributing Provenance / provenance requirements
anchors.note: Defines what provenance binds (subjects, build definition) and its completeness/accuracy requirements, delimiting what an attestation does and does not cover.

anchors.ref: in-toto Attestation Framework (Statement/Predicate/Subject model)
anchors.note: Specifies that an attestation binds named subjects via a predicate type; anything outside the modeled step/subject is unattested.

GH2-slsa-provenance-scope-gap.detection: Verify not just attestation signature validity but predicate completeness, that the deployed digest is a bound subject, that dependency resolution and pre/post-build steps are themselves attested, and that the builder ID and source URI match policy; reject artifacts whose provenance scope does not cover the full materials list (compare against an SBOM).

references.title: SLSA - Distributing provenance (v1.0)
in-toto Attestation Framework

GH2-tee-attestation-freshness-tcb-gap.mechanism: A relying party releasing secrets to a confidential-computing workload trusts a TEE remote-attestation quote (SGX/TDX/SEV-SNP) as proof the enclave is genuine and running unmodified code on a patched TCB. Two seams exist: (1) freshness, a quote must embed a verifier-supplied nonce in REPORTDATA, and a verifier that omits or fails to bind the nonce can be fed a stale-but-valid quote replayed from an earlier, healthy state; and (2) TCB recovery, the verifier must enforce a minimum TCB security-version (SVN) so it rejects quotes from a platform still at a TCB level vulnerable to a known issue that triggered an SVN bump. A verifier missing either check accepts a cryptographically valid quote that no longer reflects current enclave/platform integrity.
GH2-tee-attestation-freshness-tcb-gap.exploitation_primitive: Capture a valid quote from a healthy enclave and replay it to a verifier that doesn't check nonce freshness, or run on a downlevel-TCB platform and present a genuine quote to a verifier that doesn't enforce minimum SVN, then receive the released secrets.
GH2-tee-attestation-freshness-tcb-gap.mitre_gap: Closest is T1606 (Forge Web Credentials) by loose analogy to forging an authentication artifact, but it does not fit attestation at all: there is no forgery, the quote is genuine but stale or below the required TCB level. ATT&CK Enterprise has no technique for TEE/remote-attestation freshness replay or TCB-version downgrade against a confidential-computing verifier.

anchors.ref: Intel TDX/SGX DCAP attestation - nonce in REPORTDATA for freshness
anchors.note: Attestation flow requires a 16-byte nonce/REPORTDATA to ensure freshness and prevent replay of old quotes; verifiers must also check TCB/SVN via TCB-Recovery.

anchors.ref: intel/SGX-TDX-DCAP-QuoteVerificationService
anchors.note: Reference quote-verification service whose correct use requires enforcing TCB level and verifying report data; omission yields the freshness/SVN gap.

GH2-tee-attestation-freshness-tcb-gap.detection: Require the verifier to generate a per-request random nonce, bind it into REPORTDATA, and reject quotes lacking the expected nonce; enforce a minimum TCB SVN / collateral TCB level and reject UpToDate=false or out-of-date TCB status; bound quote validity with a short freshness window and re-attest before each secret release.

references.title: Confidential Computing 101 - DCAP Attestation overview
intel/SGX-TDX-DCAP-QuoteVerificationService

GH2-ebpf-blindspot-and-rootkit.mechanism: eBPF-based security sensors (Falco, Tetragon, EDR agents) treat the kernel event stream and the set of hook points they attach as a complete, tamper-evident view of system activity. An attacker who can load eBPF (CAP_BPF/CAP_SYS_ADMIN, privileged container) runs in the same kernel BPF subsystem and uses eBPF itself to hook getdents64, sys_read/write, tcp/socket paths, and the bpf() syscall to filter, mask, or rewrite the very events the defensive tool consumes, or attaches earlier/at a lower hook so it sees and edits data first. The sensor inherits a kernel-event view that its own substrate silently altered, and because eBPF programs do not appear as loadable kernel modules, the rootkit is inherently stealthy to module-based forensics.
GH2-ebpf-blindspot-and-rootkit.exploitation_primitive: Load a kretprobe/tracepoint eBPF program that hooks getdents64 and sys_bpf to hide your processes/files and the rootkit's own maps/programs from the EDR's eBPF sensor, or attach before the sensor's hook to rewrite events in flight.
GH2-ebpf-blindspot-and-rootkit.mitre_gap: Closest are T1014 (Rootkit) and T1562.001 (Impair Defenses), but both describe traditional kernel-mode rootkits/tool tampering. Neither captures eBPF-on-eBPF, where the attacker and defender share the same in-kernel instrumentation substrate and the security sensor's own data source is edited via hook-ordering and BPF-map tampering. ATT&CK has no eBPF-specific technique for this co-located instrumentation conflict.

anchors.ref: BPFDoor / Symbiote / LinkPro eBPF rootkits
anchors.note: Real-world eBPF malware: BPFDoor (state-linked APT), Symbiote (LatAm finance/LE), and LinkPro use getdents/sys_bpf hooking to hide processes and the BPF programs themselves; analyzed by Synacktiv and Trend Micro.

anchors.ref: Trend Micro - How BPF-Enabled Malware Works
anchors.note: Documents ebpfkit/bad-bpf/TripleCross techniques for hiding processes, filtering telemetry, and hiding BPF maps from inspection.

GH2-ebpf-blindspot-and-rootkit.detection: Inventory and alert on all loaded BPF programs and maps via bpftool / fs.bpf and the bpf() syscall audit, especially programs attaching to getdents64/sys_read/sys_bpf or attaching ahead of known sensor probes; restrict CAP_BPF and unprivileged eBPF; cross-check eBPF-derived views against an independent source (kernel memory forensics, hardware/external network capture) to spot the blind spot.

references.title: Synacktiv - LinkPro: eBPF rootkit analysis
Trend Micro - How BPF-Enabled Malware Works

GH2-workload-identity-federation-misbind.mechanism: Cloud secret brokers and workload-identity-federation trusts (AWS IAM OIDC roles, GCP Workload Identity Federation, Entra federated credentials) authorize credential exchange when an external OIDC token's issuer/audience/subject claims satisfy a trust policy. The defect is over-scoped or wildcarded subject/audience conditions, e.g. an AWS role condition matching repo:org/*:* or only matching the audience, or a sub matching any branch, so a token minted by the same trusted issuer for a different repo/branch/namespace/service account also satisfies the policy. Because the token is genuinely issued by the trusted IdP, the broker exchanges it for cloud credentials the policy never intended to grant, binding the federated identity to the wrong non-human principal.
GH2-workload-identity-federation-misbind.exploitation_primitive: From any repo/branch/namespace under the same trusted OIDC issuer, mint a legitimate OIDC token whose claims satisfy a victim role's over-broad/wildcarded StringLike condition (or a missing sub condition), then call AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity / STS exchange to obtain the victim's cloud credentials.
GH2-workload-identity-federation-misbind.mitre_gap: Closest are T1199 (Trusted Relationship) and T1528 (Steal Application Access Token), but neither captures the OIDC claim-matching seam, that a correctly-issued federation token from the right issuer can satisfy an over-scoped trust condition for the wrong workload. ATT&CK has no technique for workload-identity-federation subject/audience over-scope credential exchange; T1199 is too coarse and assumes a compromised partner rather than claim mis-binding.

anchors.ref: Tinder Security Labs - Identifying vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions & AWS OIDC Configurations
anchors.note: Misconfigured IAM trust policies let external/unaffiliated repos assume the org's AWS roles via OIDC; ships an oidc-scanner.

anchors.ref: Datadog Security Labs - No keys attached: GitHub-to-AWS keyless authentication flaws
anchors.note: Details over-permissive sub conditions and wildcard subjects enabling account takeover via OIDC federation.

GH2-workload-identity-federation-misbind.detection: Audit all OIDC trust policies for wildcards/missing sub conditions (Rezonate/Tinder OIDC checkers); require both aud and a fully-qualified sub (repo+branch+environment or full namespace/service-account); alert in CloudTrail/Cloud Audit Logs on AssumeRoleWithWebIdentity/STS exchanges whose token claims (repo, ref, actor) do not match the expected workload identity.

references.title: Tinder - Identifying vulnerabilities in GitHub Actions & AWS OIDC
Datadog Security Labs - GitHub-to-AWS keyless authentication flaws
Rezonate-io/github-oidc-checker

GH2-servicemesh-mtls-l7authz-gap.mechanism: Services in a mesh frequently gate access on the authenticated peer identity (the SPIFFE/workload SVID presented over mutual-TLS by the sidecar) and conflate that transport-level peer authentication with application-level authorization of the request's L7 content. The seam: a valid mTLS peer identity says who the calling workload is, not that this caller is entitled to the specific path/method/headers/propagated JWT/on-behalf-of claims it sent. In Istio specifically, AuthorizationPolicy defaults to allow-all when no policy exists, policies keyed on source.namespaces (rather than source.principals) can be satisfied by injecting a pod into the allowed namespace, and forwarded-identity headers (e.g. X-Forwarded-* / propagated JWTs) can be spoofed by an in-mesh workload, so a compromised/over-privileged peer reaches privileged L7 semantics the policy should deny.
GH2-servicemesh-mtls-l7authz-gap.exploitation_primitive: From a workload holding a valid mesh identity, send requests to privileged paths/methods or with spoofed forwarded-identity headers / replayed propagated JWTs that downstream services trust because the mTLS peer is authenticated, obtaining access the L7 policy never granted.
GH2-servicemesh-mtls-l7authz-gap.mitre_gap: Closest is T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud Accounts), but it models stolen/abused account credentials, not the peer-authentication-vs-L7-authorization confusion inside a service mesh. ATT&CK has no technique for authenticated-mesh-peer to L7-authz escalation, nor for spoofed propagated-identity headers within an mTLS fabric; T1078.004 misses the transport-vs-application authority gap entirely.

anchors.ref: AquilaX - Service Mesh Security: Istio mTLS Bypasses and Zero-Trust Failures
anchors.note: Documents allow-all defaults, namespace-vs-principal spoofing, and missing default-deny that let an authenticated peer exceed intended L7 authority.

anchors.ref: Istio Security concepts (authentication vs authorization)
anchors.note: Istio distinguishes peer (mTLS) authentication from request (JWT/L7) authorization; conflating them is the documented misuse.

GH2-servicemesh-mtls-l7authz-gap.detection: Require AuthorizationPolicy keyed on source.principals (SPIFFE) plus explicit path/method/JWT conditions, never source.namespaces alone; enforce a default-DENY policy; strip and re-derive forwarded-identity headers at the ingress/sidecar rather than trusting client-supplied ones; log and alert on in-mesh requests to privileged paths whose peer identity does not match the expected caller for that route.

references.title: AquilaX - Istio mTLS Bypasses and Zero-Trust Failures
Istio - Security concepts

GH2-jit-pam-approval-workflow-abuse.mechanism: Just-in-time PAM treats an elevation that passed its workflow (justification text, approver click, ticket reference) as proof of a legitimate, scoped, time-bounded grant. The seam is the workflow logic itself rather than any credential: auto-approval policies (configured to grant when approvers are unavailable or request volume is high) can be satisfied by an attacker; self-approval or approver-overlap paths let a compromised account approve its own elevation; plausible justification text social-engineers a human approver; and the activation/deactivation timing window can be raced or chained so a benign-looking JIT grant becomes standing privilege. In each case the PAM system records the elevation as legitimate while the privilege is attacker-held.
GH2-jit-pam-approval-workflow-abuse.exploitation_primitive: Trigger an elevation that routes to an auto-approval policy (or a self/peer-approval path you control), or submit a convincing justification to a human approver, then use the time-boxed grant, and chain it before it deactivates to obtain effectively standing privilege.
GH2-jit-pam-approval-workflow-abuse.mitre_gap: Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts) and T1098 (Account Manipulation), but both describe using/altering accounts, not abusing an approval-workflow's business logic to mint a legitimately-recorded elevation. ATT&CK has no technique for JIT/PAM elevation-approval abuse (self-approval, auto-approval, approver social engineering, activation-window race); the procedural-grant-as-attacker-privilege seam is unmodeled.

anchors.ref: ManageEngine PAM360 - auto-approval for access requests
anchors.note: Documents configurable auto-approval that grants elevation when approvers are unavailable or request volume is high, the abusable auto-approval path.

anchors.ref: Delinea - Just-in-Time Access
anchors.note: Describes JIT request/approval workflow model whose approval event is treated as proof of legitimate, scoped use.

GH2-jit-pam-approval-workflow-abuse.detection: Alert on elevations granted via auto-approval policy, on approver==requester or close approver-overlap, and on a high rate of just-under-threshold justification reuse; record and review session activity against the stated justification; flag JIT grants that are re-requested back-to-back to simulate standing access; require out-of-band approval for the most privileged scopes.

references.title: ManageEngine PAM360 - JIT privilege elevation / auto-approval
Delinea - What is Just-in-Time Access

GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.mechanism: A SaaS tenant evaluates each third-party OAuth grant in isolation ('App A may read my CRM') and never reasons about the transitive capability graph where App A's read scope composes with App B's external-write/outbound scope into a read-then-exfiltrate primitive neither grant alone implies. Each app holds long-lived OAuth access/refresh tokens; once one low-sensitivity integration (or its token) is compromised, the attacker reads sensitive records through App A and pushes them out through App B's sanctioned outbound scope. Because every hop uses a valid, tenant-sanctioned token, the lateral movement is indistinguishable from normal SaaS-to-SaaS automation traffic, and no single consent screen ever showed the combined authority.
GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.exploitation_primitive: Compromise one low-privilege integration token, enumerate the tenant's other sanctioned OAuth grants, and chain a read scope on App A with an outbound/write scope on App B to exfiltrate data using only valid tokens, no new consent and no credential theft.
GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.mitre_gap: Closest are T1550.001 (Use Alternate Authentication Material: Application Access Token) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but each models a single token/relationship. ATT&CK has no technique for OAuth transitive-scope toxic combinations, the composition of independently-granted scopes across multiple sanctioned apps into a capability exceeding any one consent; T1550.001 describes using one token, not chaining the scope graph.

anchors.ref: Salesloft Drift OAuth token compromise (UNC6395), Aug 2025
anchors.note: Compromised Drift OAuth tokens were used to authenticate to 700+ orgs' Salesforce instances and mass-exfiltrate data (AWS keys, Snowflake tokens); shows long-lived sanctioned OAuth tokens enabling SaaS-to-SaaS lateral data theft.

anchors.ref: AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395): why Salesforce OAuth integrations are a growing risk
anchors.note: Analysis of how sanctioned OAuth integration scopes become an exfiltration path indistinguishable from normal automation.

GH9-saas-oauth-transitive-scope-toxic-ch.detection: Build a tenant-wide OAuth grant inventory and compute the transitive scope graph to flag toxic read+external-write combinations; alert on OAuth tokens performing bulk reads followed by outbound transfers, on first-seen app-to-app data flows, and on refresh-token use from new IPs/ASNs; cap token lifetime and require periodic re-consent for high-scope apps.

references.title: Google Cloud (Mandiant) - Data theft via Salesloft Drift (UNC6395)
AppOmni - Drift breach (UNC6395) analysis

GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.mechanism: A SaaS app that exposes an inbound webhook treats the event payload ('payment.succeeded', 'user.verified', 'document.signed') as an authoritative statement of what happened in a partner system and lets it directly mutate state, rather than re-verifying against the source of truth. The exploitable conditions: endpoints with no signature; verifiers that parse-then-verify a re-canonicalized body (signature computed over different bytes than those acted on); HMAC schemes with no timestamp/nonce binding (enabling replay); and the empty-secret defect where an unset HMAC key yields a publicly computable signature. In all cases attacker-supplied data crosses into the application's control plane, flipping entitlements, marking orders paid, or promoting accounts, without the receiver pulling current state back from the originator.
GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.exploitation_primitive: POST a forged or replayed event (exploiting a missing/empty signing secret, or a verifier that signs the raw body but acts on a re-parsed one) to flip a state-changing flag, e.g. credit quota/mark-paid/promote-account, directly via the webhook.
GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.mitre_gap: Closest are T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1565.001 (Data Manipulation: Stored Data Manipulation), but neither captures webhook-as-control-plane, where an untrusted inbound assertion is wired directly to a state mutation absent re-verification. ATT&CK has no technique for forged/replayed webhook events as a first-class control-plane abuse; T1190 implies a code-level exploit, whereas this is trusted-by-design event ingestion.

anchors.ref: CVE-2026-41432 (QuantumNous/new-api < 0.12.10)
anchors.note: Empty StripeWebhookSecret default lets an attacker compute valid signatures and forge payment webhooks to credit arbitrary quota without payment; concrete webhook-as-control-plane forgery.

anchors.ref: Stripe webhook signature/timestamp tolerance docs
anchors.note: Documents the timestamp-in-signature and 5-minute tolerance that prevent replay; absence of these checks is the abuse condition.

GH9-inbound-webhook-as-control-plane.detection: Reject webhooks lacking a configured signing secret (fail closed on empty secret); verify HMAC over the exact raw bytes acted upon and enforce a timestamp-tolerance + nonce/idempotency-key to block replay; for any value-releasing event, re-fetch authoritative state from the originating API before mutating; alert on webhook events that drive entitlement/financial state changes without a matching origin-API confirmation.

references.title: GHSA-xff3-5c9p-2mr4 / CVE-2026-41432 - Stripe webhook signature bypass via empty secret
Stripe - Receive Stripe events in your webhook endpoint

GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.mechanism: iPaaS platforms (Zapier/Make/Workato) let any recipe/scenario in a workspace execute under the workspace's shared, pre-authorized connections, so a flow author inherits the full authority of every connection an admin once added, dozens of long-lived OAuth grants to business-critical systems, without the platform re-checking that this particular automation/author/trigger was authorized for that specific connection's scope. The stored OAuth tokens become an ambient capability any flow can borrow. One workspace compromise (a low-privilege builder account, an over-shared workspace, or a malicious imported template) inherits the union of every connected SaaS at once, because attaching an existing connection to a new flow requires no fresh consent.
GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.exploitation_primitive: With author/edit rights in the workspace, create or import a flow that attaches an existing privileged shared connection and use it to pivot into systems you were never individually granted, the connection's stored tokens execute the flow with the owner's authority.
GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.mitre_gap: Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1199 (Trusted Relationship), but neither models iPaaS shared-connection authority, the platform-mediated borrowing of one principal's stored OAuth grant by an unrelated automation. ATT&CK has no technique for shared-connection privilege fanout where the automation layer re-uses ambient credentials across flows; T1078.004 assumes possession of the account, not inheritance via a shared connection object.

anchors.ref: Obsidian Security - Integration Security for SaaS Applications
anchors.note: Notes that compromise of an iPaaS account inherits OAuth tokens for every connected app and that individually-safe integrations form toxic combinations when chained.

anchors.ref: Zapier - Manage app connections / managed apps
anchors.note: Documents the shared-connection model where flows execute under workspace connections.

GH9-ipaas-shared-connection-privilege-fa.detection: Inventory shared connections and which flows reference them; alert when a new/edited flow attaches a privileged connection or when an imported template references existing connections; bind connections to specific automations/owners and require re-authorization to attach a connection to a new flow; monitor connected-SaaS audit logs for actions from iPaaS service principals that don't match the flow's expected scope.

references.title: Obsidian Security - SaaS integration security best practices
Zapier - Manage app connections with managed apps

GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.mechanism: Every SaaS app a user is logged into trusts that requests carrying its session cookies/bearer tokens originate from the user's own intent in-page, while a browser extension's declared host permissions entitle it to read and act inside those same authenticated origins. An extension therefore silently inherits the user's live, MFA-satisfied sessions to every site its permissions cover. A turned-malicious extension (silent post-install update, acquired/compromised developer account, or a sleeper) uses chrome.cookies / scripting / declarativeNetRequest to harvest session cookies and bearer tokens and to drive in-page actions, impersonating the user across M365/Workspace/etc. without touching the login flow, so account takeover happens entirely after authentication and behind MFA. Bidirectional cookie injection (chrome.cookies.set) lets the attacker replay the stolen session into their own browser.
GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.exploitation_primitive: Ship/acquire an extension with broad host permissions that periodically reads session cookies/tokens for target SaaS origins and exfiltrates them (or injects them into an attacker browser), inheriting MFA-satisfied sessions without any credential phishing.
GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.mitre_gap: Closest are T1539 (Steal Web Session Cookie) and T1185 (Browser Session Hijacking), which are the nearest fit, but they do not model the browser-extension permission grant as the standing, post-auth authority that inherits every covered SaaS session, nor the supply-chain turn (silent update / acquired developer account) that flips a benign extension malicious. The ambient-authority-via-host-permissions seam is only partially captured.

anchors.ref: Socket - five malicious Chrome extensions hijacking enterprise sessions (Jan 2026)
anchors.note: 2,300+ installs; extensions requested cookies/scripting/declarativeNetRequest across Workday/NetSuite/SuccessFactors, harvested auth cookies, and offered bidirectional cookie injection for full account takeover post-MFA.

anchors.ref: Varonis - Cookie-Bite: Chrome extension steals Microsoft session tokens, bypasses MFA
anchors.note: Demonstrates an extension stealing M365 session tokens to maintain cloud access behind MFA.

GH9-webextension-authenticated-session-i.detection: Enforce extension allowlisting via browser enterprise policy and block broad host permissions / cookie access for non-vetted extensions; monitor for extension auto-updates that add permissions; detect session-token use from new device/IP fingerprints inconsistent with the user's browser (token-binding / continuous access evaluation); alert on impossible-travel reuse of a session cookie across two concurrent browsers.

references.title: CSO Online - Five Chrome extensions caught hijacking enterprise sessions
Varonis - Cookie-Bite

GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.mechanism: An enterprise treats its telemetry pipeline (OpenTelemetry/Datadog/Fluent collector) as benign plumbing with broad host/network read reach and permission to egress to whatever exporter destinations its often remotely-managed config names. The seam is the remote-config channel (OpAMP) plus the collector's standing egress trust: an attacker who can push a config, via the OpAMP management server, a poisoned config repo, or a modified collector image, adds an attacker-controlled exporter, turning the always-trusted, egress-permitted agent into a sanctioned exfiltration channel, and simultaneously inserts processors that drop/filter security-relevant signals (e.g. removing the filelog receiver for audit logs), blinding the SOC during the operation. The pipeline's 'just observability' provenance suppresses scrutiny of its destinations and omissions.
GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.exploitation_primitive: Push a collector config via OpAMP (or a poisoned config repo) that adds a second exporter pointing at attacker infrastructure and a filter/processor that drops alerting-relevant spans/logs, exfiltrating telemetry while blinding detection.
GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.mitre_gap: Closest are T1567 (Exfiltration Over Web Service) and T1071.001 (Application Layer Protocol: Web Protocols), but these describe generic egress, not the trusted-telemetry-pipeline-as-exfil-channel via remote config, combined with the dual signal-suppression that blinds the SOC. ATT&CK has no technique for observability-collector remote-config abuse (rogue exporter + dropped security signals) where the egress is pre-sanctioned plumbing.

anchors.ref: Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Configuration Security: Hardening the OpAMP Trust Boundary
anchors.note: Describes pushing an OpAMP config to add a secondary exporter for immediate telemetry exfiltration and to silence specific alert pipelines (remove filelog receiver / drop alerting spans).

anchors.ref: OpenTelemetry OpAMP specification
anchors.note: Open Agent Management Protocol governs remote config push to collectors, the trust boundary being abused.

GH9-observability-collector-egress-remot.detection: Allowlist exporter destinations and treat any new destination as an auditable PR-gated change; run a dedicated security-critical collector with no OpAMP supervisor so it cannot receive remote config; require mTLS with a private CA on the OpAMP channel and reject unknown management servers; alert within seconds on collector config-change events and on telemetry egress to non-allowlisted endpoints, and detect drops in expected security signal volume.

references.title: Systems Hardening - OTel Collector Remote Config Security (OpAMP)
OpenTelemetry - Open Agent Management Protocol (OpAMP)

GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.mechanism: Calendar-integrated AI meeting bots (Otter, Fireflies, Read, Fathom) treat any event on a user's calendar as a legitimate invitation to attend, record, and transcribe, so they auto-join on the user's behalf via a highly permissive OAuth grant to the calendar, and downstream the org trusts the bot's transcript/summary as authoritative meeting truth feeding CRM notes, action items, and agent workflows. Two abuse paths: an injected calendar event (open invite, spoofed organizer, or compromised scheduling integration) summons a victim's note-taker into a meeting it was never meant to attend, silently exfiltrating the conversation; and content seeded into the meeting/transcript can steer the AI summary so attacker-chosen instructions or false 'agreed' decisions flow into the downstream systems that ingest the bot's output as trusted record (a prompt-injection-style data-to-control crossing).
GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.exploitation_primitive: Inject or spoof a calendar invite that triggers the victim's auto-join bot to attend and transcribe a meeting, exfiltrating the conversation; or seed transcript text engineered so the AI-generated summary/action items carry attacker-chosen 'decisions' into CRM/automation.
GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.mitre_gap: Closest are T1566 (Phishing) and T1114.002 (Email Collection: Remote Email Collection), but neither captures calendar-injection-as-summons of an auto-join AI agent, nor the downstream trust placed in AI-summarized transcripts as authoritative record. ATT&CK has no technique for AI-agent auto-join eavesdropping or transcript-summary poisoning; T1114 is email-scoped and misses the agentic meeting-capture and downstream-ingestion seam.

anchors.ref: Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes: the growing risk of AI meeting assistants
anchors.note: Describes auto-join via permissive OAuth calendar grants, viral re-invite dark patterns, and bots joining every future meeting; 800 accounts in 90 days at one customer.

anchors.ref: Fireflies.ai AI note taker
anchors.note: Representative calendar-integrated auto-join meeting bot whose transcripts feed CRM/action-item workflows.

GH9-meeting-bot-autojoin-transcript-iden.detection: Govern AI-notetaker OAuth grants (least-privilege calendar scope, block auto-add-to-all-meetings); alert on bot participants joining meetings, especially externally-organized or recently-injected events; require host admit/consent for recording bots; treat AI-generated summaries as untrusted input, do not auto-execute action items into CRM/automation without human confirmation, and watch for instruction-like text in transcript-derived fields.

references.title: Nudge Security - Shadow AI is taking notes (AI meeting assistants)
University of Cambridge - Prevent AI note-taking bots from joining Teams meetings

GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.mechanism: Enterprise systems attribute actions performed under a user identity to that human's intent and presence, while RPA platforms run attended/unattended bots that hold and replay human or human-equivalent credentials to drive those same systems through the UI. Unattended robots must log into Windows and target apps with stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator's credential store (native vault or Azure Key Vault/HashiCorp integration), so the target grants the bot the impersonated identity's full authority and records every action as that person. An attacker who compromises the RPA orchestrator, a bot's credential vault, or an unattended runner harvests these standing human-equivalent credentials and drives privileged business actions through the sanctioned UI automation, and because the activity is attributed to the human, malicious actions are non-repudiably blamed on an innocent user and blend into expected automation traffic.
GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.exploitation_primitive: Compromise the RPA orchestrator or its credential store (or a runner host) to retrieve the stored human-equivalent credentials/tokens, then drive privileged business transactions through the bot's UI automation under the victim's identity.
GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.mitre_gap: Closest are T1555 (Credentials from Password Stores) and T1078.002 (Valid Accounts: Domain Accounts), but neither models the RPA-orchestrator/credential-vault as a concentration of standing human-equivalent identities, nor the non-repudiation collapse where bot actions are attributed to a human who was not present. ATT&CK has no technique for RPA bot-identity impersonation or unattended-runner credential harvesting as a business-process abuse.

anchors.ref: UiPath Orchestrator - unattended robot credentials & credential stores
anchors.note: Unattended robots require stored user credentials retrieved from the orchestrator vault (native, Azure Key Vault, HashiCorp) to log into Windows and target apps, the concentrated standing-credential surface.

anchors.ref: UiPath Robot ICAM credentialing guidance
anchors.note: Vendor guidance on credentialing robots and managing their access, confirming bots hold human-equivalent credentials.

GH9-rpa-unattended-bot-human-credential-.detection: Give each robot a distinct, attributable non-human identity rather than a shared human credential; alert on orchestrator credential-asset retrievals and on robot logons outside scheduled windows/hosts; baseline per-bot action patterns and flag privileged transactions diverging from the automation's defined workflow; monitor for human-attributed sessions whose source is an RPA runner during the user's off-hours.

references.title: UiPath Orchestrator - Integrating credential stores
MITRE ATT&CK T1555 - Credentials from Password Stores

GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.mechanism: A business process trusts the 'agreement completed/signed' signal from an e-signature workflow (DocuSign Connect callback or a status read) as a final legal fact and wires it to release value automatically: provisioning access, paying an invoice, triggering the next agreement, or archiving the authoritative copy. The seam is the gap between 'a completion event arrived' and 'the intended parties validly agreed to this content'; an attacker subverts the completion signal rather than the per-page cryptographic seal, by forging or replaying the completion webhook (e.g. missing/weak HMAC, no timestamp/replay protection), manipulating signer-routing/recipient logic so an unauthorized party satisfies the signing step, or racing a document/template swap before the downstream system fetches the completed artifact, so the receiving system releases value on a procedurally invalid completion even though each tamper-sealed page checks out.
GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.exploitation_primitive: Forge/replay the DocuSign Connect completion webhook (or manipulate recipient routing / swap the template before fetch) so the downstream system treats the envelope as validly completed and releases value (access, payment, next-step trigger).
GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.mitre_gap: Closest are T1190 (Exploit Public-Facing Application) and T1565.001 (Stored Data Manipulation), but neither captures completion-event-as-value-release trust, the business-logic seam where a procedurally-invalid 'completed' signal triggers downstream value despite valid page seals. ATT&CK has no technique for e-signature completion-callback forgery/recipient-routing abuse; T1190 implies a code exploit, this is trusted-event business-logic abuse.

anchors.ref: DocuSign Connect webhooks / HMAC verification
anchors.note: Connect completion events drive downstream provisioning/order/payment automation; vendor guidance requires HMAC verification plus replay protection (timestamp/unique ID), absence of which enables forgery/replay.

anchors.ref: eSignGlobal - DocuSign Connect: securing webhooks with HMAC signature verification
anchors.note: Details HMAC verification and replay-protection practices for completion callbacks; the abuse condition is their omission.

GH9-esignature-completion-event-value-re.detection: Verify the Connect HMAC over the raw body and enforce timestamp/idempotency replay protection; never act on the callback alone, re-fetch envelope status and the completed artifact (by envelope ID and document hash) from the e-signature API before releasing value; validate recipient-routing/signer identity against the intended parties; alert on completion events whose envelope/document hash differs from the originally-sent document.

references.title: DocuSign Connect (webhooks)
eSignGlobal - Securing DocuSign Connect webhooks with HMAC

GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.mechanism: Low-code/no-code platforms (Microsoft Power Platform) let a flow built by any citizen developer run under whatever connection reference is attached to it, so when an admin shares a privileged service connection (or a flow) into a maker environment, every automation referencing it executes with that connection's elevated authority on the assumption the maker was implicitly entitled to it. This indirect (implicit) use of a connection circumvents the maker's own access rights, and the platform logs the action under the service connection identity rather than the human who directed it. A citizen developer, an attacker who took over a maker account, or a planted shared flow/template can author an automation that references the admin's privileged connection to read restricted data, write to production, or create identities, silently elevating a low-privilege author to the connection owner's authority. DLP policy historically enforced only on new connections, leaving large back-catalogs of existing shared connections exploitable.
GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.exploitation_primitive: As a low-privilege maker (or via a compromised maker account / planted shared template), build a flow that references an admin's shared privileged connection and use its stored authority to perform actions beyond your own rights; the action is logged as the connection, not you.
GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.mitre_gap: Closest are T1078.004 (Valid Accounts: Cloud) and T1098 (Account Manipulation), but neither models low-code shared-connector authority borrowing, where the platform executes a flow with a connection owner's privilege regardless of the author's rights, and attribution is to the connection not the human. ATT&CK has no technique for citizen-dev privileged-connector borrow / implicit-connection privilege escalation.

anchors.ref: Secureworks (CTU) - Power Platform Privilege Escalation
anchors.note: Demonstrates privilege escalation via the Power Platform API by elevating an existing service principal; shows the platform as a privilege-escalation target.

anchors.ref: Zenity Labs - The Long and Winding Road of DLP Patches in Power Platform
anchors.note: Documents that DLP restrictions were enforced only on new connections, not existing ones, leaving shared connections borrowable.

GH9-citizen-dev-flow-privileged-connecto.detection: Inventory shared connections/connection references and which flows and makers consume them; alert on a flow referencing a connection whose owner differs from the author, and on newly imported shared flows/templates; enforce DLP on existing (not just new) connections; correlate connected-system audit logs for service-connection actions that exceed the directing maker's own entitlements.

references.title: Secureworks - Power Platform Privilege Escalation
Zenity Labs - DLP Patches in Power Platform
